spike_name: batch-size sweep against the unnest path (TA-11 prelim — informs whether heap_multi_insert is worth implementing)

context.rationale: Isolates the bulk-insert mechanic from WAL writing + partition routing so we can tell which dominates total insert_ms. If the dominant cost was the prepared INSERT itself, this number would be close to LUBM-1's insert_ms; the gap reveals what heap_multi_insert can vs cannot address.

default.note: current loader.rs BATCH_SIZE constant — SWEET SPOT

gap_components_likely: WAL writing (UNLOGGED skips it entirely; dominant in the real path)
partition routing (real _pgrdf_quads is LIST-partitioned by graph_id; flat target is not)
real triple values vs sequential synthetic ids (negligible at this scale)

verdict: TA-11 (heap_multi_insert) addresses neither dominant cost component at LUBM-1 scale. Sweet spot of BATCH_SIZE=1000 is already at ~0.40 us/triple in the bulk-insert mechanic — going to 1-shot 100k batch only saves 6.7 ms vs 100 batches of 1000. SPI per-batch overhead is well-amortized at BATCH_SIZE>=1000 (per_batch_us = 400 us at default, of which the actual INSERT row work is the bulk; SPI dispatch itself is single-digit us). heap_multi_insert's prospective wins (skipping planner/executor, ~few us/batch) are dwarfed by WAL+partition routing costs that it cannot address. **Recommendation for TA-9**: TA-11 LOW priority. The Track A perf wins land via TA-D3 (-17% e2e dict batching) + TA-D2 (-54% e2e shmem prewarm). Insert path is already near-optimal modulo deployment-level GUCs (synchronous_commit, wal_level) which are not extension code. Document TA-11 as MEASURED NOT WORTH IMPLEMENTING and proceed to TA-10 (COPY BINARY — different mechanism, separately measure).
